title: SQL — Talking to Databases
subtitle: The language databases understand

sections.content:
You know that databases store data in organized tables. But how do you actually get data in and out? You use **SQL** (Structured Query Language).

SQL is a special language designed for one purpose: communicating with databases. You write a SQL query, the database reads it, and sends back the results. It's like placing an order at a restaurant — you tell the kitchen what you want, and they bring it to you.
keyPoints: SQL stands for Structured Query Language (pronounced "sequel" or "S-Q-L")
It has simple English-like commands: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE
SQL works with almost all relational (table-based) databases
You don't need to memorize complex syntax — the basics are surprisingly readable

sections.instruction: Here are the four essential SQL commands. Each one does exactly what its name suggests.

blocks.label: Update a user
blocks.code:
UPDATE users SET email = 'new@email.com'
WHERE name = 'Sara';

sections.explanation: Notice how readable SQL is — it almost reads like English. SELECT means "give me," FROM means "from this table," WHERE means "but only the rows that match this condition." You don't need to be a programmer to understand what these queries do.

sections.instruction: Match each SQL keyword to the action it performs.

sections.content:
These four commands — SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE — are often called **CRUD** operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete). They cover the vast majority of what apps do with databases.

Every time you sign up for a service, that's an INSERT. Every time you scroll your feed, that's a SELECT. Every time you edit your profile, that's an UPDATE. And when you delete your account, well, that's a DELETE.
sections.tip: You don't need to write SQL from scratch. Claude Code can generate SQL queries for you — just describe what data you want in plain English, and it will write the query.

completionMessage: You've learned SQL, the language for talking to databases. With just four commands — SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE — you can do almost anything with stored data. Next, we'll zoom out and look at how frontend and backend work together.
